coredata.dc:title: Preparation of n-doped carbon quantum dots for the detection of PA in real explosion dust samples
coredata.dc:creator: Wu, Xiaojing
Li, Hongda
coredata.dc:description: Nitrogen doping carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as a carbon and nitrogen source via the water hydrothermal method. The size of the obtained N-CQDs is about 5.51 nm, and FTIR results show that their surface contains many –OH and –NH2 groups. N-CQDs can selectively recognize PA and its homologous (DNP and NP) in the presence of other analytes (including TNT, Tetryl, PETN, RDX, HMX, NC, NG, DNT, NT, NB, DNP, NP, BP, PC, AN, Ca2+, Cl-, Mg2+, Na+, H2O2). A linear correlation is also observed between PA concentration (0 ∼ 10.0 μmol/L) and N-CQDs fluorescence intensity (0.05 mg/mL) at 556 nm. The linear curve is y = -3.64 × 107x + 404.91, and the detection limit is ∼ 70 nmol/L. The Stern-Volmer graphs lack sufficient linearity, confirming the static quenching interaction between N-CQDs and PA. Finally, the N-CQDs are successfully applied to determine PA content in simulated and real samples quantitatively.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon quantum dots
Picric acid
Explosive dust
Detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Optical detection of PA by N-CQDs
Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity
Selectivity and anti-interference of N-CQDs for PA detection
Kinetic analysis of N-CQDs for PA
Fluorescence detection of PA by N-CQDs

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: TEM (a) and HRTEM (b) image of the synthesized pristine N-CQDs. (c) FTIR spectrum of pristine N-CQDs. Insite: particle-size distribution of pristine.
XPS spectra: (a) survey, (b) C 1 s core level, (c) N 1 s core level, (d) O 1 s core level of the N-CQDs.
UV–vis absorption (left) and fluorescence (right) spectrum of N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL). Excitation wavelength: 427 nm.
The 3D fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL).
Effect of different pH on the fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs and N-CQDs for PA.

Fluorescence intensities (λex/λem = 427/556 nm) of N-CQDs in the presence or non-presence for various analytes (1 for N-CQDs, 2 ∼ 22 for PA, TNT, Tetryl, PETN, RDX, HMX, NC, NG, DNT, NT, NB, DNP, NP, BP, PC, AN, Ca2+, Cl-, Mg2+, Na+, H2O2).
Kinetic analysis of N-CQDs for PA.
(a) Fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs for different concentrations of PA；(b) Linear calibration curve. Inset: the Stern-Volmer graphs.
the analysis result of simulated samples.
The analysis result was compared with the national standard method.

head.ce:title: Preparation of n-doped carbon quantum dots for the detection of PA in real explosion dust samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Institute of Functional Molecules, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology; National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Development of Boron and Magnesium Resources and Fine Chemical Technology; Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Green Functional Molecular Design and Development, Shenyang 110142, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Shenyang University of Chemical Technology; National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Development of Boron and Magnesium Resources and Fine Chemical Technology; Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Green Functional Molecular Design and Development

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Institute of Functional Molecules, Shenyang University of Chemical Technology; National-Local Joint Engineering Laboratory for Development of Boron and Magnesium Resources and Fine Chemical Technology; Liaoning Province Key Laboratory of Green Functional Molecular Design and Development, Shenyang 110142, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Forensic Chemistry, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Shenyang 110035, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Criminal Investigation Police University of China

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Forensic Chemistry, Criminal Investigation Police University of China, Shenyang 110035, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Nitrogen doping carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) are synthesized using o-phenylenediamine as a carbon and nitrogen source via the water hydrothermal method. The size of the obtained N-CQDs is about 5.51 nm, and FTIR results show that their surface contains many –OH and –NH2 groups. N-CQDs can selectively recognize PA and its homologous (DNP and NP) in the presence of other analytes (including TNT, Tetryl, PETN, RDX, HMX, NC, NG, DNT, NT, NB, DNP, NP, BP, PC, AN, Ca2+, Cl-, Mg2+, Na+, H2O2). A linear correlation is also observed between PA concentration (0 ∼ 10.0 μmol/L) and N-CQDs fluorescence intensity (0.05 mg/mL) at 556 nm. The linear curve is y = -3.64 × 107x + 404.91, and the detection limit is ∼ 70 nmol/L. The Stern-Volmer graphs lack sufficient linearity, confirming the static quenching interaction between N-CQDs and PA. Finally, the N-CQDs are successfully applied to determine PA content in simulated and real samples quantitatively.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) have attracted much attention due to their exceptional optical properties and wide applications in optoelectronics, drug delivery, bioimaging, and chemical sensing.[1–3] Unlike semiconductor quantum dots containing S element and heavy metals, CQDs possess good biocompatibility, high chemical stability, good water solubility, convenient synthesis, and easy functionalization. CQDs are mainly synthesized by “top-down” and “bottom-up.”[4–6] The “top-down” approach is obtaining small-size CQDs from high-mass carbon sources, resulting in complex morphology, uniformity, irregular size, and shape. However, CQDs synthesized by “bottom-up” methods employ small-sized carbon materials, such as molecules or ions) possess uniform composition and less deformity. Many methods have been developed to synthesize CQDs, including hydrothermal, microwave treatment, ultrasonic, carbonization, and pyrolysis. The hydrothermal method requires abundant raw materials, simple experimental equipment, and low cost; therefore, it is the most commonly used method to prepare CQDs in laboratories.

Picric acid (PA) was an essential explosive component during the Second World War since its explosive performance was about 105 % higher than TNT [7]. PA has been widely used in rocket fuel, fireworks, dye industry, preservative preparation, and drug synthesis. Consequently, large amounts of PA are released into the environment, causing severe soil and water pollution. The PAs’ substantial biological toxicity can cause headaches, weakness, anemia, and liver damage [8]. Additionally, the natural degradation rate of PA is prolonged due to its structural characteristics [9]. Recently, several detection methods, such as high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [10], ion migration spectrometry (IMS) [11], surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy (SERS) [12], and electrochemical detection [13] have been developed for PA detection. However, these methods usually require expensive equipment, complex sample pretreatment, and long assay times. Optical detection methods [14–22] have the advantages of simple operation, low cost, fast response speed, good selectivity, high sensitivity, strong visualization, and is often used to visualize the amount of PA detected. CQDs surface has rich functional groups, such as –OH, –COOH, –NH2, etc.), which can bind and interact with several analytes to induce energy transfer and produce noticeable optical changes, demonstrating simple, rapid, and highly sensitive analysis characteristics. Therefore, it is widely used in food detection, drug analysis, environmental monitoring, and bioassay. Studies on PA detection via CQDs have been in-depth and extensive. However, most CQDs have drawbacks of pure organic medium, low sensitivity, or low affinity, resulting in low sensitivity to complex real samples. Therefore, developing new CQDs that can rapidly visualize and intelligently analyze PA at the incident site is essential.

PA is an electron-deficient system that can easily bind to electron-rich systems based on donor–acceptor (D-A) interaction. When the CQDs contain an electron-enriching unit, they interact with PA, thereby inducing energy transfer. Therefore, a new type of carbon quantum dots is prepared using a hydrothermal method employing o-phenylenediamine as a carbon and nitrogen source. Based on the PA fluorescence quenching effect on novel CQDs, a fluorescence probe for PA detection is constructed, and a new method for rapid and efficient PA detection is developed for real sample analysis and biological environment monitoring.

O-phenylenediamine, 2,4-dinitrotoluene (DNT), nitrotoluene (NT), nitrobenzene (NB), 2,4-dinitrophenol (DNP), nitrophenol (NP), CaCl2, MgCl2, NaCl, H2O2 were purchased from the Energy Chemical Ltd. China. 2,4,6-trinitrophenol (PA), 2,4,6-trinitrophenylmethylnitramine (Tetryl), 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), pentaerythritol tetranitrate (PETN), hexogen (RDX), octogen (HMX), nitroglycerine (NG), nitrocellulose (NC), black powder (BP, the main inorganic components is KNO3), pyrotechnic compound (PC, the main inorganic components are Ba(NO3)2 and KClO3 or KClO4), ammonium nitrate explosives (AN, the main inorganic components is NH4NO3) were sourced from the explosives sample library of Criminal Investigation Police University of China.

o-phenylenediamine (6.0 g, 0.056 mol) and distilled water (30.0 mL) were mixed in a polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle, and the hydrothermal reaction was performed at 180℃for 12 h. After cooling the polytetrafluoroethylene reaction kettle to room temperature, the dark green product was filtered through a 0.22 μm filter. The filter liquor was dialyzed (MW: 1500 Da) in deionized water for 24 h, removing the small particles. Finally, the dark green solution was freeze-dried to obtain a gray powder of N-CQDs.

Nicolet iS50 Fourier transform infrared spectrometer (Agilent Technologies, USA) was used to analyze the functional groups on N-CQDs surface, in a scanning range of 400 ∼ 4000 cm−1. TECNAI 10 transmission electron microscope (TEM) (Philips-FEI Corporation, Netherlands) was used to observe the microscopic morphology of N-CQDs at an accelerated voltage of 200 kV. The optical properties were studied by UV-3100 UV–visible spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Company, Japan) and RF-5301PC fluorescence spectrophotometer (Shimadzu Company, Japan). All the characterizations and relevant test data are provided in the Electronic Supporting Information(ESI†).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical detection of PA by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The N-CQDs stock solution (5 mg/L) was prepared in DMSO diluted to 0.4 mg/L with PBS (10 mM, pH 7.0, 10 % DMSO). The stock solution (1 × 10-2 mol/L) of interfering analysts (organic explosives: PA, TNT, Tetryl, PETN, RDX, HMX, NC, NG, DNT, NT, NB, DNP, NP, inorganic explosives: BP, PC, AN and common ions and oxidants: Ca2+, Cl-, Mg2+, Na+, H2O2) were in DMSO and distilled water and distilled water, respectively. The UV–vis absorption scanning range was 300 ∼ 700 nm, the fluorescence spectra scanning range was from 400 ∼ 800 nm, and the excitation wavelength was 568 nm for measurements. All the slit widths were 5 nm at room temperature (about ∼ 22℃).

The simulated PA samples were prepared by adding different concentrations (0.01, 0.03, 0.05, 0.07, and 0.09 mg/g) inwashed sand. Real samples were obtained from the scene of a real explosion case in 2020, and the explosion dust was kept in the material evidence sample bank of the Criminal Investigation Police University of China. The simulated and real case samples were weighed (2.0 g), dissolved in DMSO (1.0 mL), stirred at room temperature for 20 min, and filtered to obtain the test solution.

The factors affecting the formation of N-CQDs (temperature and time)are optimized (Figures S1 and S2). Increasing the temperature and prolonging the reaction time effectively improves the degree of carbonization. However, too high a temperature or extended time can also cause the oxidation agglomeration of CQDs, reducing the quantum yield. Thus, the optimal reaction temperature is set at 180℃, and the reaction time at 12 h. The fluorescence quantum yield of synthesized N-CQDs at these conditions is 28.31 %[16,23], as shown in Table S1.

The morphology and microstructure of carbon quantum dots are characterized by TEM, HRTEM, FT-IR, Raman and XPS (Figs. 1 and 2
). TEM images reveal that the prepared N-CQDs are spherical without aggregation, demonstrating good dispersion. The size of N-CQDs is mainly distributed in the 4 ∼ 7 nm range (Fig. 1a Inset), with an average particle size of 5.51 nm. HRTEM image (Fig. 1b) revealed lattice fringes of graphitic carbon (sp2) with a spacing of 0.23 nm, indicating the crystalline nature of the prepared N-CQDs.[24] The prepared N-CQDs are also characterized by FTIR (Fig. 1c). The absorption peak at 3374 cm−1 indicates O-H stretching vibration. The absorption peaks at 3287 cm−1 and 3193 cm−1 are attributed to N-H stretching vibrations. The absorption peaks at 3027 cm−1 and 1536 cm−1 indicate C-H stretching vibration and C = C skeleton vibrations on the benzene ring, respectively. The absorption peak at 1275 cm−1 is assigned to the C-O stretching vibration. The absorption peak at 1159 cm−1 illustrates a C-N asymmetric stretching vibration. The FTIR spectra suggest that the N-CQDs' surface contains abundant hydrophilic groups, such as amino and hydroxyl groups, significantly contributing to the improved water solubility. Moreover, several amino groups are conducive to enhancing the interaction between N-CQDs and PA. For the Raman spectrum, the Z ratio (ID/IG) of D-band and G-band intensity of carbon point is 1.06, which indicates that NCQDs contains both sp2 carbon clusters and sp3 defects[25], and has a partially disordered graphite structure (Fig. 2).

The XPS spectrum (Fig. 3 a) shows three sharp and strong peaks at 284.9, 400.9, and 531.9 eV are observed, which are indicative of the XPS peaks of C 1 s, N 1 s, and O 1 s, respectively. The major elemental composition was carbon (74.14 %), followed by nitrogen (22.35 %) and oxygen (3.51 %). The fitting of the XPS of C 1 s core level region (Fig. 3b) suggests the observation of three sub-peaks at 284.7 eV, 285.4 eV, and 286.5 eV, corresponding to the C = C (sp2)/C-C (sp3), C-N, and C-O bonds, respectively.[26] The XPS of N 1 s (Fig. 3c) indicates that there are four sub-peaks at 398.8 eV, 399.3 eV, 399.9 eV and 401.0 eV, which are assigned to the pyridinic N, amino N, pyrrolic N, and graphitic N bonds, respectively.[27] These results revealed the presence of sp2 C and amide bonds. In addition, the XPS of O 1 s (Fig. 3d) were observed two sub-peaks at 531.8 eV, and 532.8 eV, which were assigned to C = O and C-O bonds, respectively.[28] Therefore, the major elemental composition and functionalized group analyses imply that the synthesized materials were nitrogen-doped CQDs with multiple surface bonds.

The optical properties of N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) are also studied using absorption and fluorescence. As shown in Fig. 4 (left), the absorption spectrum of N-CQDs denotes a very wide absorption band at 350–500 nm. A distinct absorption peak at 427 nm is attributed to n→π*[29] transition of heteroatom unsaturated groups, combined with the redshift of absorption bands caused by auxochrome (–OH and –NH2). At an excitation wavelength of 427, the fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs is tested. A strong emission peak appears at 556 nm, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 (right). A 3D fluorescence spectrum of carbon quantum dots is presented in Fig. 5 . The results show that N-CQDs (0.05 mg/ml) has no correlation with the excitation wavelength in the range of 320 ∼ 500 nm, The non-excitation dependence reflects the uniformity of the N-CQDs.[30] The highest intensity is demonstrated at an excitation wavelength of ∼ 425 nm, then decreases gradually as it shifts to higher wavelengths. Finally, the optimal excitation wavelength is determined at 427 nm, combined with the absorption spectrum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Effect of pH on the fluorescence intensity
ce:section.ce:para: To explore the pH effect for PA detection by N-CQDs, the fluorescence intensity under different pH values (2 ∼ 11) is studied. Buffer solutions with different pH values are added to N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) solutions and mixed N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) for PA (10.0 μmol/L) solutions. Fluorescence intensity is monitored at 556 nm with an excitation wavelength of 427 nm, and results are shown in Fig. 6 . The fluorescence intensity of N-CQDs is greatly affected by strong acids, demonstrating a strong fluorescence signal in a weak acid and base (pH 6 ∼ 8). This is attributed to the weak NH2 and –COOH protonation on the N-CQDs surface. The N-CQDs' fluorescence intensity in the presence of PA remains almost the same in the pH range of 2 ∼ 11, attributed to the quenching effect. Finally, the best test solution was selected as the PBS buffer solution containing 10 % DMSO (pH = 7.0).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and anti-interference of N-CQDs for PA detection
ce:section.ce:para: PA selective recognition ability of N-CQDs is investigated in the presence of different analysts (organic explosives: PA, TNT, Tetryl, PETN, RDX, HMX, NC, NG, DNT, NT, NB, DNP, NP; Inorganic explosives: BP, PC, AN; Common ions and oxidants: Ca2+, Cl-, Mg2+, Na+, H2O2) and the experimental results are shown in Fig. 7 . The N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) fluorescence intensity exhibits significant quenching at 556 nm in the presence of PA (10.0 μmol/L), DNP (50.0 μmol/L), and NP (50.0 μmol/L) with a quenching efficiency of 87 %, 92 %, and 96 %, respectively. N-CQDs contain many N atoms that can bind to acidic phenolic hydroxyl groups, inducing fluorescence quenching. However, the N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) fluorescence intensity at 556 nm is barely changed in the presence of other analysts (TNT, Tetryl, PETN, RDX, HMX, NC, NG, DNT, NT, NB, DNP, NP, BP, PC, AN, Ca2+, Cl-, Mg2+, Na+, H2O2 for 50.0 μmol/L). These results illustrate N-CQDs' good selectivity to explosives containing phenolic hydroxyl groups (PA, DNP, and NP).

To explore the PA detection (10.0 μmol/L) in the presence of coexisting explosives with similar structures (including DNP, NP, TNT, Tetryl, PETN, RDX, HMX, NC, NG, DNT, NT, NB, DNP, NP, BP, PC, AN, Ca2+, Cl-, Mg2+, Na+, H2O2 for 50.0 μmol/L), the fluorescence spectra of N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) are measured and the results are shown in Fig. 7. When PA is detected in the presence of molecules with structural similarities, the N-CQDs' fluorescence intensity at 556 nm still presents a noticeable quenching trend. These results reflect that N-CQDs can selectively identify PA without being affected by other explosives with similar structures.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Kinetic analysis of N-CQDs for PA
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of PA (3, 8, and 10 μmol/L) and N-CQDs solution (0.05 mg/mL) are mixed to explore the influence of reaction time between PA and N-CQDs solution. The fluorescence intensity change is immediately measured at 556 nm (Fig. 8 ). The N-CQDs' (0.05 mg/mL) fluorescence intensity decreases rapidly and tends to be stable within 180 min when the different concentrations of PA are added. The results show that the PA determination by N-CQDs is rapid and stable.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence detection of PA by N-CQDs
ce:section.ce:para: The fluorescence spectrum of N-CQDs and different concentrations of PA are shown in Fig. 9 a. The N-CQDs (0.05 mg/mL) fluorescence intensity at 556 nm decreases continuously with increasing PA concentration from 0 ∼ 10 μmol/L. Further investigation reveals that the fluorescence intensity has a linear relationship with PA concentration in the 0 ∼ 10 μmol/L concentration range. The linear equation y = -3.64 × 107x + 404.91 and its linear correlation coefficient R2 is 0.9992 (Fig. 9b). According to the expression, the limit of detection is 3σ/Ks [31] (the standard deviation of the blank sample (σ) is 8.35, the slope of linear equation (Ks) is 3.64 × 107), the calculated limit of detection (LOD) for PA is ∼ 70 nmol/L, illustrating that this method possesses promising detection potential for trace PA at the scene of explosive cases. The fluorescence quenching mechanism could be divided into dynamic and static quenching. Static quenching is generally due to the formation of complex molecules. In contrast, the dynamic quenching is associated with the collision. The predicted fluorescence quenching mechanism of NCQDs for PA is a static quenching. Therefore, the Stern-Volmer graphs [32] in Fig. 9a-inset lack linearity and validate the static quenching nature of the interaction between N-CQDs and PA.

A new type of carbon quantum dot (N-CQDs) is synthesized employing a hydrothermal method using o-phenylenediamine as a carbon and nitrogen source. The N-CQDs are characterized by transmission electron microscopy (TEM), Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Raman spectroscopy, X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), UV–Vis spectrophotometry, and fluorescence spectroscopy. The results show that the average size of synthesized N-CQDs is about 5.51 nm. The carbon quantum dots' surface contains many hydroxyl and amino groups, and the maximum absorption wavelength of N-CQDs is 427 nm. The maximum emission wavelength of N-CQDs is 556 nm. The N-CQDs exhibit a specific recognition ability for PA, DNP, and NP in PBS solution (10 mmol/L, pH 7.0, 10 % DMSO). The PA concentration (0 ∼ 10.0 μmol/L) is linearly correlated to the N-CQDs fluorescence intensity at 556 nm, and the detection limit is ∼ 70 nmol/L. In addition, the Stern-Volmer graphs lack linearity, confirming the static quenching interaction between N-CQDs and PA. Finally, the proposed method is validated against the national standard method (standard name: GB/T 5750.8–2006) by analyzing PA in the real case of explosion dust.
Xiaojing Wu: Writing – original draft. Hongda Li: Writing – review & editing, Validation, Supervision, Project administration, Methodology, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21804140), Science and Technology of the Ministry of Public Securityof the People's Republic of China (No. 2022JC02), the Shenyang Youth Science and Technology Innovation Talent Support Program (No. RC220480), Project of Applied Basic Research Program from Liaoning Province (No. 2023JF12/101300097)

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.microc.2024.110573.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
